steps.1: Case Study-MFA. We start with patient status because it gates the entire downstream logic — a suspected case skips localisation entirely, reducing the number of questions the user has to answer. This keeps the flow short for the most common triage scenario.
steps.2: Pathogen type determines which options appear on the next screen. C. difficile is always GI-tract, so localisation adds no value there and is skipped automatically. The groupings (MRSA/Other, 3MRGN/4MRGN/VRE) reflect the actual isolation categories used in clinical practice.
steps.3: Localisation only appears for confirmed or contact patients with MRSA/MRGN/VRE — the combinations where site actually changes the recommended PPE. Airway localisations trigger respiratory protection requirements that wound or urine sites do not.
steps.4: The planned activity is the single biggest driver of PPE requirements. A brief handshake-level contact needs very different precautions than an invasive procedure. We kept the categories broad to avoid decision paralysis.
steps.5: Aggravating factors (coughing, incontinence, dementia) can escalate PPE requirements even when the base pathogen/activity combination would normally require less. This step catches edge cases that a lookup table would miss.
steps.chat: The chat interface lets users ask free-form follow-up questions after the structured intake. The wizard context is passed invisibly so the assistant already knows the full situation — users should not have to repeat themselves.
